overview: Michael Ovitz explains how Creative Artists Agency replaced Hollywood's studio-controlled labor model with talent-controlled packages. Founded by five young William Morris agents in 1975 with $100,000, CAA used predictable television commissions to fund expansion into film. Instead of selling scripts, actors, or directors separately, it assembled complete projects and made studios compete for distribution rights. Jurassic Park became the fullest expression: Crichton, Spielberg, Kennedy, financing terms, and ownership arrived as one scarce, inseparable package.

CAA's advantage was organizational as much as contractual. Teams shared every client, information flowed through meetings, reports, readers, and the mailroom, and the agency paid generously to retain talent. That system reduced dependence on any single agent and let CAA combine clients across disciplines. Ovitz later applied the same cultural literacy and preparation to Coca-Cola advertising and the Sony and Matsushita studio acquisitions. Yet his Disney failure exposed the model's boundary: an autonomous packager who aligns independent parties may struggle inside a hierarchy controlled by another leader.

takeaways.title: Packaging moved leverage to creative talent
takeaways.detail: CAA refused to sell material naked. By combining scripts, directors, actors, and producers before approaching a studio, it turned fragmented inputs into a unique product. Studios still supplied capital, marketing, and distribution, but could no longer swap individual creators or dictate every term.

takeaways.title: Television cash flow financed film ambition
takeaways.detail: CAA began television-only because union rules paid performers weekly, producing reliable commissions. The founders repaid their initial $100,000 debt within six months and avoided further borrowing, then used the stable base to support film projects whose development and backend economics took years.

takeaways.title: Teams converted relationships into institutional assets
takeaways.detail: Traditional agents guarded clients as personal property. CAA surrounded each client with several agents, allowing relationships to survive interpersonal friction and junior people to inherit responsibility. Shared economics and information made cross-client packages possible and helped keep losses to roughly five or six clients over 25 years.

takeaways.title: Cultural research expanded the addressable market
takeaways.detail: CAA treated magazines, art, technology, and global business as forward indicators rather than decoration. That research supported bets such as Shogun, a demographic-specific Coca-Cola campaign producing 35 commercials for the former cost of seven, and relationships that connected Japanese electronics companies with Hollywood studios.

takeaways.title: Autonomy was both strength and constraint
takeaways.detail: Ovitz excelled when he could align talent, money, and distribution without answering to an owner. At Disney, overlapping authority with Michael Eisner made that operating style destructive. CAA also proved difficult to diversify or capitalize because its shared culture and privately held economics resisted allocating ownership among a larger organization.

chapters.title: Jurassic Park demonstrates the complete package
chapters.summary: Ovitz helps Michael Crichton through writer's block, gets Steven Spielberg committed before publication, builds a budget and producing team, then gives Universal 24 hours to accept a partnership rather than sell the studio isolated rights.
chapters.quote: the bad news is you don't own it

chapters.title: Agents turn loyalty into long-duration optionality
chapters.summary: Years of uncompensated support for Crichton look inefficient until one idea becomes a franchise. Ovitz frames persistence as an agent's core duty, showing why trust and creative renewal cannot be optimized transaction by transaction.
chapters.quote: It was my job to be there for him

chapters.title: CAA treats studios as distributors
chapters.summary: The agency never offers an unassembled project. Owning the only combination of desired script, director, and stars lets clients negotiate as partners while studios compete to finance, market, and distribute the finished concept.
chapters.quote: we viewed the studios as distribution entities, period

chapters.title: Media literacy becomes a commercial capability
chapters.summary: Ovitz requires agents to scan roughly 100 magazine titles because editors and art directors anticipate culture months ahead. The agency uses that awareness to judge material, build relationships, and challenge the assumption that only buyers understand audiences.

chapters.title: Coca-Cola buys audience-specific creative scale
chapters.summary: CAA argues that one national commercial cannot serve daytime, prime-time, and late-night audiences equally. It pitches 35 tailored concepts and ultimately delivers 35 spots for what Coke had spent on seven.
chapters.quote: we're going to do 30 to 40 commercials a year

chapters.title: A generational disagreement creates CAA
chapters.summary: Young William Morris agents object when leadership celebrates signing an aging star instead of pursuing contemporary film talent. The dispute reveals incompatible views of where culture and the agency business are heading.

chapters.title: Weekly television pay funds a debt-free launch
chapters.summary: CAA starts with $100,000 and focuses on television because clients are paid every Thursday. Fast commissions repay the loan in six months and create a stable base before the founders dedicate resources to slower film development.

chapters.title: Client teams replace agent fiefdoms
chapters.summary: Multiple agents cover each artist, so one strained relationship does not cost the firm a client. The model institutionalizes trust, trains successors through live relationships, and opens paths to combine writers, actors, and directors across departments.

chapters.title: Shared knowledge makes packaging repeatable
chapters.summary: The team system demands dense communication, readers, reports, meetings, and mailroom discipline. Because agents know the whole roster, combinations such as De Niro, Pacino, and Michael Mann become organizational products rather than lucky introductions.

chapters.title: Corporate finance protects the buyer ecosystem
chapters.summary: Fearing studio failures would eliminate CAA's customers, Ovitz builds Japanese relationships and helps arrange Sony's purchases of CBS Records and Columbia, then Matsushita's acquisition of MCA Universal.
chapters.quote: I needed people that are buyers with money

chapters.title: Disney exposes the autonomy mismatch
chapters.summary: Ovitz leaves CAA expecting to recreate its culture at Disney, but shared leadership with Michael Eisner becomes direct conflict. He later recognizes that his preference for independence made him poorly suited to being an employee.
chapters.quote: I'm not a very good employee

chapters.title: Succession prioritizes institutional survival
chapters.summary: CAA's founders sell to the next generation for a reported $200 million financed from future profits. Ovitz accepts delayed payment because preventing partners from splintering matters more than maximizing immediate proceeds.
chapters.quote: I only cared about the business staying alive

title: CAA with Michael Ovitz: How Packaging Rewired Hollywood
